segments.s344-g121: نماند is the negative past form of ماندن ('to remain') meaning 'did not remain' or 'was not left'. به کس uses the dative/benefactive به + کس = 'for anyone' (here indefinite). روز سختی is a noun phrase 'a day of hardship' (روز + genitive-like modifier سختی). The following نه رنج continues the negation in a list ('nor suffering'), with نه repeating to link another negative item.
segments.s344-g122: نه ... و ... نه ... strings negative items in a series: نه آسانی 'nor ease', و شادمانی 'and rejoicing', نه گنج 'nor treasure'. The coordinating و simply links آساني and شادماني as a pair within the negative series.
segments.s344-g123: بد و نیک 'bad and good' is a coordinated subject; بر ما is a prepositional phrase 'upon/us'. همی is an older literary marker that gives a habitual/continuing sense ('always/indeed') and combines with بگذرد (3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form of گذشتن) to mean 'pass over' or 'befall'. Word order (subject — oblique phrase — aspect marker — verb) is typical of classical Persian narration.
segments.s344-g124: نباشد here is a negative jussive/optative form ('let there not be' / 'should not be') used to give a prescriptive or hortatory sense. دژم is the predicate adjective 'sullen' or 'grim'. The relative clause هرکه دارد خرد ('whoever has wisdom') follows and specifies the person referred to, so the whole gives: 'Whoever has wisdom should not be sullen.'
segments.s344-g65: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb (classical Persian often puts the indirect object first). گفت is the past tense 'said' but the provided sense list lacks the verbal sense (so that sense is not available). نستاو is an archaic contraction equivalent to 'نیست او' ('he is not'). زین ('this saddle') + در ('in/onto') + بگرد (imperative 'turn' or 'make turn') form a small clause: literally 'do not/is not — turn this saddle on/into him' in the speech; the imperative بگرد governs the preceding locative phrase.
segments.s344-g66: تو is the address 'you' (subject); ایدر is a locative adverb 'here' modifying the predication; غریبی and وبی‌پای are nominal/adjectival predicates linked to مرد ('man') that describe the person addressed. The conjunction و joins the two attributes; the whole phrase reads as a nominal sentence 'You, here, (are) a stranger and a footless/helpless man.'
segments.s344-g67: This is a triadic list (بیابان و دریا و اسپان) followed by the adjective یله modifying اسپان: 'the desert and the sea and the loose/unrestrained horses.' Word order is the normal Persian coordination with adjectives after the coordinated noun they modify.
segments.s344-g68: به here is the dative/target marker 'to/for'; ناآشنا is 'an unfamiliar/strange person'; چون functions as an interrogative 'how' (classical use): 'چون سپارم ...?' 'how shall I entrust...?'; سپارم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of سپاردن used in a rhetorical question 'shall I entrust'; گله is the object 'flock/herd'.
segments.s344-g69: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (classical past); گشتاسپ is the subject's name; غمگین is a predicative adjective 'sorrowful'; برفت is an archaic past form of رفتن ('he departed/left')—the provided sense list does not include this verbal meaning.
segments.s344-g70: ره 'the way/route' is the object taken; ساربانان قیصر is a genitive/possessive sequence 'the caravan‑leaders of the Caesar' (owner after the possessed noun), and گرفت is the past verb 'he took/seized' agreeing with the implied subject (he). The whole clause means 'he took the road followed by the Caesar's caravan‑leaders.'
segments.s344-g71: یکی is the indefinite 'someone/a certain person'; آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction where آفرین (an acclamation/praise) + کرد ('made/did') = 'praised/cheered'; بر introduces the target with the preposition 'upon/to' and ساربان is the addressee 'the caravan‑leader.'
segments.s344-g72: که here introduces the content/wish that follows ('that' / 'may'); پیروز is 'victorious'; بادی is an archaic optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may you be' (not the lexical 'windy' given in the list), so the provided lexical senses do not match the verbal/optative use; و is 'and'; روشن‌روان is a compounded epithet 'bright‑souled/cheerful' functioning as the second wished quality.
segments.s344-g105: مرا گر بداری تو یاری کنم is a conditional clause plus a main verb. مرا گر بداری literally 'if you give/take me' (مرا = 'me' as object placed before the verb for emphasis; گر = 'if'), where بداری is 2nd-person present/subjunctive from داشتن used here in the sense 'take/have/accept'. تو is an emphatic subject 'you' (poetic/thou). یاری کنم uses یاری 'help' as object/complement and کنَم (1st-person present/subjunctive) to express the speaker's willingness/future action 'I will help.'
segments.s344-g106: برین = بر + این 'on/over this' (written together as برین) introducing the object being taken charge of. پتک و سندان = 'the hammer and anvil' (coordinated nouns, پتک first). سواری کنم is a light verb construction: سواری (riding/being the rider/attendant) + کنم (1st-person) meaning 'I will ride/attend/serve' → idiomatically 'I will take charge/handle' those tools.
segments.s344-g107: چو بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو = temporal 'when'; بشنید is past/preterite 'heard'). بوراب is the subject proper name. ز + و represent the clitic sequence ز او = 'from him' (z + pronoun); here they appear as separate items but function together as 'from him'. داستان = 'tale/story' the thing heard. The clause order is typical older/poetic Persian: temporal marker + verb + subject + source + object.
segments.s344-g108: به یاری او گشت همداستان = 'he became like-minded/ally with him.' به یاری = 'into/for help' (here introduces the relation 'as an ally'); او is the pronoun 'him'. گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became'. همداستان functions as predicate meaning 'in agreement/ally' (literally 'same-tale' = like-minded/accomplice).
segments.s344-g109: گرانمایه گویی = 'noble/precious utterance' (گرانمایه adjective + گویی 'speech/utterance'). به آتش بتافت combines به + آتش 'into the fire' and بتافت (preterite) from tافتن meaning 'leapt/was thrown/moved' — a poetic verb here indicating sudden motion or kindling: 'leapt into the fire' or 'caught fire.' The verb is in past narrative tense.
segments.s344-g110: چو شد تافته سوی سندان شتافت = 'when it became tafafta (radiant/ignited), it hastened toward the anvil.' چو شد = 'when it became'; تافته is used figuratively from the root تافتن meaning 'radiant/ablaze' (here adjectival/predicative 'blazing'). سوی = 'toward'; شتافت is past of شتافتن 'to hasten/rush.' The clause links the ignition to the rushing motion.
segments.s344-g111: به گشتاسپ دادند پتکی گران = 'they gave (دادند) a heavy hammer (پتکی گران) to Gushtāsp (به گشتاسپ).' Word order is Verb-final in the clause (دادند after the indirect object marker به + name), with the theme (پتکی گران) following the verb as is common in epic diction.
segments.s344-g112: برو انجمن گشته آهنگران describes the smiths' location/state: برو here is an older form 'went/are gone' (go), انجمن 'assembly/meeting,' گشته is the participle/past 'having become/gone' (perfective participle), and آهنگران 'the blacksmiths.' Together it reads as a circumstantial clause: 'the smiths having gone to the assembly.'
segments.s344-g97: ورا is a dative prepositional pronoun 'to/for him' marking whose companions these are; the noun phrase یار و شاگرد 'companion and apprentice' names the people, joined by و 'and'. بد is an adjective 'bad' describing those people, and the following numeral (سی و پنج) enumerates them. Word order is classical Persian: possessor/locative (ورا) + nominal group + numeral.
segments.s344-g98: ز ... ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by' repeated for both instruments; پتک و ز آهن names the tools ('by the sledgehammer and by the iron'). رسیده is a past participle/predicative 'having come / brought to' and the phrase رسیده به رنج means 'brought to / come to suffering' (رنج = hardship). Overall, this clause qualifies the companions as made miserable by hammer and iron.
segments.s344-g99: به + دکانش marks motion/position 'into/at his shop' (دکان with possessive -ش 'his'). بنشست is the simple past 'sat down' (3sg). گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name). دیر here is an adverb 'for a long time' indicating the duration of the sitting.
segments.s344-g100: شد is the past of شدن 'became'. آن پیشه‌کار 'that tradesman' is the subject. از + نشستنش (the gerund/nominalized verb 'his sitting' with enclitic -ش) expresses the cause/reason, and سیر is an adjective 'sated / weary of' — so شد ... سیر = 'became tired/sated of his sitting.'
segments.s344-g101: بدو is the dative 'to him' (marks the addressee), گفت is the past 'said' (introducing direct speech), and آهنگر‑ای is a vocative 'O blacksmith' (‑ای marking address). نیکخوی is an epithet 'good‑natured' modifying the vocative. The clause functions as 'he said to him, “O good‑natured blacksmith”'.
segments.s344-g102: چه is the interrogative 'what'. داری is 2nd‑person present 'you have / you want' (here 'what do you want'). به دکان ما is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in/at our shop' (ما as possessive 'our'), and آرزوی ('wish') is the nominal object — 'what wish do you have at our shop?' i.e. 'what do you want here?'.
segments.s344-g103: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. داد پاسخ is a verb + noun collocation 'gave an answer' (i.e. 'answered'). که introduces the following direct speech, and ای (attached as که‌ای in the text) is the vocative particle 'O', so که‌ای نیک‌بخت is the spoken address 'O fortunate one'.
segments.s344-g104: نپیچم is negative + subjunctive/imperative‑like form 'I do not/will not turn/avoid' from پیچیدن (here 'to turn aside, shrink away'). سر 'head' is the direct object ('turn my head away'). از ... پتک 'from the hammer' marks the thing avoided, and وز here functions as a fused classical form 'و ز' = 'and from', so وز کار سخت completes the list 'and from the hard work' (کار سخت = hard work). The whole sentence is a resolute refusal to shirk the hammer or hard labor.
segments.s344-g17: ز introduces a temporal/causal clause here ('when' / 'upon'). هیشوی is a proper name (Hishoy) in this context, although the provided lexical senses list only adverbial meanings; treat it as the person who heard. بشنید is perfect/past 'he heard' (3rd person); گشتاسپ is the subject (Gushtasp); گفت is the reportative verb 'said' linking this clause to the following reported content.
segments.s344-g18: که introduces reported speech ('that'). از تو is a partitive/agent phrase 'by you' or 'from you'; مرا is the indirect object ('to me' / 'from me' in the sense of possession); نیست is the negative copula 'is not' (here past-relative: 'was not/has not been'); چیزی is the indefinite 'anything/something'; نهفت is adjectival/predicative 'hidden/concealed'. Together: 'that you have not hidden anything from me.'
segments.s344-g19: ز من marks the source/topic 'as for me' or 'from me'. هرچ خواهی is a free relative meaning 'whatever you want' (خواهی = 2nd person present of خواستن 'to want'). ندارم is the negative of داشتن 'I do not have', here idiomatically 'I do not have (any) reluctance' or 'I will not withhold'. دریغ functions as the noun 'reluctance/hesitation' that ندارد negates.
segments.s344-g20: ازین = از + این ('from/of this' or 'these'), here introducing an enumerated list of items offered. The nouns افسر, مهر, دینار, تیغ are linked by و 'and' and are the things being offered (helmet, seal, dinars, sword).
segments.s344-g21: ز دینار is partitive/measure 'of dinar' (i.e. some dinar). لختی here means 'a little' / 'a small amount' (an adverbial/quantifying word). به هیشوی is the dative 'to Hishoy'. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (3rd person).
segments.s344-g22: ازان هدیه is a prepositional phrase 'from that gift' (giving the cause). شد is the copular/verb 'became' (3rd person past). مرد گیرنده is a noun phrase 'the man who received (it)' where گیرنده is a present-participial adjective 'receiver/recipient'. شاد is the resulting predicate 'happy/joyful'.
segments.s344-g23: ز کشتی here marks location/scene ('on/at the ship'). سبک modifies بادبان and means 'light' (small or light-weight sail). برکشید is a prefixed verb (بر + کشید) 'hoisted/raised' in the past tense (3rd plural implied). The clause describes the action of setting sail.
segments.s344-g24: جهانجوی را is the definite object marked with را ('the world-seeker' as the one moved). سوی قیصر is the directional phrase 'toward Caesar'. کشید is the past tense of کشیدن 'to pull/steer/bring' (here 'steered/drew toward'); the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s344-g81: "بدو ساربان گفت کای شیرمرد" = word‑for‑word: 'to him the caravan‑leader said, O brave man.' بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the subject; ساربان is the subject 'the caravan‑leader' who is speaking; گفت is the verb 'said' (here reported speech). کای is an archaic/vivid vocative particle 'O' and شیرمرد is the addressed epithet 'brave man' (direct address).
segments.s344-g82: نزیبد ترا هرگز این کارکرد: نزیبد is an older/stylistic stative verb meaning 'befit' (here with negative force in context: 'does not befit'). ترا (split into ت + را in the text) is the second‑person object 'you' (ت = 2nd‑person suffix, را = object marker). هرگز 'never' is the adverb modifying the verb phrase. این کارکرد = 'this action/deed' (این = 'this'; کارکرد = 'act/doing'). Word order is somewhat flexible/poetic: the predicate 'نزیبد' is stated and the beneficiary/object ترا is marked by را; the whole means 'This action never befits you.'
segments.s344-g83: به چیزی که ما راست چون سر کنی: به introduces the instrumental/goal 'by/through something'; چیزی 'something' is the head noun; که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'. ما راست = 'we truly' (راست used adverbially = 'truly/rightly'); چون سر کنی = 'when/as you make (your) head' — سر here means 'head/chief' and کنی is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/become'. The clause gives the condition 'by something whereby you truly become our chief.'
segments.s344-g84: به آید‌گر آهنگ قیصر کنی: here به again introduces direction/aim ('toward'); آهنگ + قیصر = 'intention/aim toward the Caesar (the emperor)'. کنی is the 2nd‑person verbal form 'you make' = 'you set/you intend'. آیدگر is an older/poetic noun meaning something like 'one who goes/visitor' or more generally a verbal/nominal element tied to motion; in context the phrase functions as 'if you set your intention to go to the emperor/Caesar.'
segments.s344-g85: ترا بی‌نیازی دهد زین سخن: ترا (ت + را) is the 2nd‑person object 'you' (ت as suffix, را the object marker). بی‌نیازی 'self‑sufficiency/independence' is the thing given. دهد is the 3rd‑person singular present 'gives' (here 'will give/make'). زین سخن is an archaic/poetic locution 'by these words' / 'from these words' (زین = 'from/of this' + سخن 'speech/words'), indicating the means by which you will be made self‑sufficient.
segments.s344-g86: جز آهنگ درگاه قیصر مکن: جز 'except'; آهنگ 'intention/aim' and درگاه قیصر 'the court/presence of the Caesar' form a genitive phrase 'the intention toward the Caesar's court.' مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' (2nd person). Together: 'Do nothing except set your mind toward the emperor's court.'
segments.s344-g87: و‌گر گم شدت راه دارم هیون: و‌گر 'and if' (conditional introducer). گم 'lost' is the predicate/adjective. شدت here represents an older/poetic verbal element related to شدن 'to become' (forming 'if you become/are lost'); this is archaic morphology rather than the modern separate auxiliary. راه 'road/way' sets the context ('on the road'/'in the way'). دارم 'I have' (present). هیون 'beast/pack‑animal' (poetic for camel/horse). Overall: 'And if you are lost on the road, I have a beast (for you).' Note: some forms (شدت) are archaic/poetic and reflect older verb morphology.
segments.s344-g88: پسندیده و مردم رهنمون: پسندیده 'pleasing/approved/chosen' is an adjective describing the beast (i.e. a suitable or well‑chosen animal). و 'and'. مردم 'people' here = 'folk' or 'men' (travelers/assistants). رهنمون is a noun meaning 'guide(s)/guidance'. The phrase supplies companions/guides: 'a suitable beast and people as guides.'
segments.s344-g49: چو here is a subordinating/time word ('when'); بشنید is past tense 'he heard' with the subject following (گشتاسپ). The phrase دل پر ز درد is a poetic construction meaning 'heart full of pain' (دل + پر 'full (of)' + ز 'from/of' + درد 'pain'), with پر functioning as an adjective-like modifier and ز marking source/reason.
segments.s344-g50: This clause is inverted word order: ز دیوان (from the divan/court) + بیامد (came) + دو رخساره زرد (two pale visages). بیامد is the simple past 'came'; رخساره زرد is a noun + adjective (pale/wan) describing the faces/complexions.
segments.s344-g51: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain / a' introducing an event; باد سرد 'a cold wind' is a metaphorical image. از جگر 'from the liver' uses جگر poetically as the seat of feeling; برکشید is a past-tense, transitive/ intransitive verb 'rose up / was drawn up' describing a sudden chill or pang.
segments.s344-g52: This is a locative phrase + subject + verb: به نزدیک چوپان ('to/near the shepherd') precedes the subject قیصر and verb رسید ('arrived/reached'), so the whole means 'the Caesar approached the shepherd.' رسید is simple past 'arrived/approached.'
segments.s344-g53: This is an archaic possessive/name copula order: 'جوانمرد را نام نستاو بود' = 'the young man's name was Nastāv.' را here is a topical/pertinence marker attached to the possessor; نام is 'name'; بود is the past copula 'was.' The token نستاو functions here as a proper name (no matching sense in the provided senses list, so mapped as unknown).
segments.s344-g54: A simple predicate list joined by و: دلیر و هشیوار و با تاو بود — 'he was brave and alert and with radiance.' با + noun (تاو) indicates possession/quality 'with radiance'; بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s344-g55: به نزدیک نستاو is a prepositional/locative phrase 'near Nastāv' placed before the clause. چون شد فراز is a temporal clause 'when/when he became up/on high' (چون here functions as a subordinating 'when/as'), with شد 'became' in the past and فراز 'on high/aloft' indicating elevation or approach. Overall this sets the time/place: 'when he came close to Nastāv.'
segments.s344-g56: آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase 'he made praise / he praised' (آفرین here = 'bravo, praise' and کرد = past 'did'); the clause continues with و (and) + بردش نماز, where بردش is a verb 'took/led him' and نماز is the object 'prayer.' In context the sense is 'he praised him and led him to prayer.' Two items (برو and بردش) have no fully matching senses in the provided lists and are treated as irregular/unknown here.
segments.s344-g113: This clause has two coordinated past verbs in classical narrative style. بزد is a past third-person singular ('he struck'), with پتک as its direct object; then و links to بشکست ('he broke/shattered') which takes سندان ('anvil') as its object. The final و introduces گوی which here is a noun ('a word/saying') that continues into the next clause.
segments.s344-g114: ازو ('from/of him/it') gives the source or topic; گشت is past 'became' (literally 'turned into' / 'came to be'); بازار is the subject/scene 'the bazaar/market'; پر is an adjectival complement 'full (of)'; گفت‌وگوی is the noun 'conversation/talk' in a genitive/construct sense modifying 'پر' (full of talk). The verb گشت governs the predicate 'بازار پر گفت‌وگوی' ('the bazaar became full of talk').
segments.s344-g115: بترسید is a past/preterite form meaning 'he was afraid' or 'he feared' (classical narrative). The proper name بوراب is the subject; و connects to the speech report. گفت here functions as the verb 'he said' (the provided lemma senses list lacked the verbal sense, so that verbal sense is not in the supplied sense list). ای جوان is a vocative: 'O young man!'
segments.s344-g116: به زخم تو is a prepositional phrase 'against/to/with regard to your wound' (به + noun + possessive تو). آهن is the subject 'iron' (poetically: metal/weapon); ندارد is the present/past-tense negative of 'have' used here as the main verb 'does not have'; توان is a nominal complement 'power/ability' — together 'iron has no power against your wound.' Note توان is a noun here, not an auxiliary.
segments.s344-g117: This is a correlative negative list introduced by repeated نه ... نه ('neither ... nor'). The items are tools/elements of smithing: پتک (sledge), آتش (fire), سندان (anvil) and دم (literally 'breath', here 'bellows' / the blowing of the fire). No finite verb appears in this fragment; it lists what was absent.
segments.s344-g118: چو + past verb forms a temporal clause: چو بشنید 'when he heard'. Note classical word order: چو (when) + بشنید (he heard) + گشتاسپ (Gushtasp) as subject. زان ('from that/thereupon') introduces the result clause; شد دژم is a copular construction 'he became sullen/grim' (شد = 'became', دژم = adjective).
segments.s344-g119: بینداخت is a past transitive 'he threw/cast down' (compound classical form of انداختن); پتک is its object. و links the next result clause: بشد + adjective (گرسنه) is a light-copular past construction meaning 'he became/grew (hungry)'.
segments.s344-g120: Again a correlative negation with two short phrases joined by نه ... نه. روی خورش is a nominal phrase 'his face toward the stew/food' (روی = face; خورش = dish/stew) and بد is the adjective 'bad/ugly' describing that attitude — together meaning 'he did not make a bad face at his food.' جای بنه is another nominal phrase 'place for the pile/serving' (جای = place; بنه here is a noun), negated by the second نه. In short, the structure negates two reactions toward the food.
segments.s344-g41: دبیران is the plural subject 'the secretaries'. که introduces a relative clause 'who...', and بودند is the past plural copula 'were'. در بارگاه is a prepositional phrase 'in the court/palace' modifying where they were; the word order is subject — relative clause — verb — location (typical in Persian narrative).
segments.s344-g42: همی here marks a continuous/habitual action ('kept/was'), and کرد is the past main verb used with the noun نگاه to form the compound verb 'نگاه کردن' (to look); هریک 'each one' is the subject of this verb, and به دیگر is the directional/reciprocal phrase 'at one another'.
segments.s344-g43: کزین is a prepositional compound 'from this', introducing an instrument/source. کلک پولاد is a noun–noun phrase 'steel pen' (pen made of steel). گریان شود is a two-word verbal phrase: گریان (causing to weep) plus شود (the present/future/passive auxiliary 'become/will be'), so the sense is 'will be made to weep' or 'will cause to weep'.
segments.s344-g44: همان روی قرطاس is a noun phrase 'that very face/surface of the paper' (همان intensifies/points to the exact surface). بریان شود uses بریان 'scorched/burnt' with شود as a stative/resultative auxiliary: 'shall be scorched.' The clause is parallel to the first and describes the effect on the paper.
segments.s344-g45: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at one time / in a single instant / suddenly'. باید is the modal 'must / it is necessary'. به زیرش is a prepositional phrase 'from beneath him' (زیر + pronominal suffix ـش). بلند here expresses the upward/lifting idea ('aloft' / 'raise'); in context it functions as the verbal notion 'be lifted' though the verbal auxiliary is condensed in style.
segments.s344-g46: The repeated preposition به marks instruments or means ('by/with'): به بازو کمان 'by the arm, the bow' and به زین بر کمند 'and by the saddle onto the noose'. و simply joins the two instrument phrases; بر before کمند marks position 'on/upon' (i.e. onto the noose). The noun phrases lack an ezafe because of the terse poetic diction but are read as genitive/compound units.
segments.s344-g47: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice / aloud' (به used adverbially + آواز). گفتند is the past third-person plural 'they said'. ما را is the object phrase 'to us' with the definite/object marker را, and دبیر follows as the word being addressed or mentioned ('scribe/secretary'); the ordering reflects direct speech being introduced.
segments.s344-g48: زیانست is a nominal-predicate construction 'it is harmful / it is a loss'. پیش آمدن is an infinitival/gerundial verbal phrase 'to come / to occur' (پیش here part of the compound meaning 'to happen/come forth') and ناگزیر modifies it as 'inevitable'. Together the clause states as a fact that 'coming/arrival is unavoidable' and that this is harmful.
segments.s344-g1: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. The noun گشتاسپ is the subject; نزدیک دریا is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ('near the sea') following the subject, and رسید is the past tense verb 'reached/arrived' placed at the end, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s344-g2: پیاده شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'he dismounted/went down (to foot)'; پیاده here 'on foot' with شد (past of شدن) forming the action. The coordinating و links the two verbs, and باژ خواهش بدید is a noun phrase object: باژ 'a cry/noise' qualifying خواهش 'a request/entreaty', followed by بدید (past of دیدن) 'he saw/noticed' placed after its object in this poetic ordering.
segments.s344-g3: یکی ... بود is the existential/indefinite pattern 'there was a certain ...'. پیرسر is the head noun 'old man/gray‑headed one' and بود is the past copula 'was'. هیشوی نام appears to give the personal name; here هیشوی is used as the name but that specific sense is not listed in the provided senses (see note). نام 'name' follows as the label.
segments.s344-g4: A string of attributive adjectives and nouns joined by و describe the old man: جوانمرد 'chivalrous/honourable', بیدار 'alert/awake'. با introduces the coordinated nouns رای و کام 'counsel/plan and desire', so the phrase lists qualities of the named elder.
segments.s344-g5: آفرین کرد is a light‑verb construction where آفرین (exclamation/praise) takes کرد (did) to mean 'offered praise' or 'exclaimed “bravo”'. برو here is an interjection/imperative form used idiomatically as part of the exclamation. The subject گشتاسپ follows the compound verb in this poetic order; و گفت then links to the reported speech that follows.
segments.s344-g6: که introduces the content of what was said ('that'/'that [may it be]'). با + جان + پاکت is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with your pure soul' — پاکت is پاک 'pure' with the enclitic ‑ت 'your'. خرد is the noun 'wisdom'; باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)' and جفت here means 'mate/companion', so the clause expresses a wish: 'May wisdom be paired with your pure soul.'
segments.s344-g7: ازایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' placed before the noun; یکی introduces an indefinite 'one/a certain'. نامدارم functions as an adjectival phrase 'renowned/famous' (the final -م may reflect an older/poetic form or possessive clitic in context), and دبیر is the head noun 'secretary/scribe'. The overall order is prepositional phrase, indefinite, adjective, then noun.
segments.s344-g8: A coordinated string of epithets modifying the scribe: خردمند 'wise', روشن‌دل 'bright‑minded/clear‑hearted', and یادگیر 'one who learns/who is quick to learn'. The conjunctions و simply link these qualities in a typical Persian coordinate list.
segments.s344-g73: خردمند is the subject 'the wise man'. چون introduces a temporal/as clause ('when'/'as'), here functioning like 'when'. روی گشتاسپ is a noun phrase 'the face/appearance of Gushtasp' (روی = face), and دید is the simple past verb 'saw' that closes the clause. Word order is subject + temporal subordinate + object phrase + verb, typical of epic Persian.
segments.s344-g74: پذیره شد is a passive/predicative phrase 'was received/was welcomed' (پذیره = reception/acceptance; شد as auxiliary/passive). و links two coordinated predicates. جایگاهش 'his place/seat' (جایگاه + possessive -ش) is the object of گزید, where گزید is the past tense 'chose/selected'. The subject (the wise man) carries over to both verbs.
segments.s344-g75: This is a compact descriptive cluster: سبک 'light (in weight)' modifies whatever was spread; باز here has the adverbial sense 'open/wide'; گسترد is the past verb 'he spread'; and گستردنی is a participial/adjectival form related to 'able to be spread' or 'spread-out', a poetic reiteration of the spreading action. The phrase uses repetition and adjectival stacking typical of epic diction.
segments.s344-g76: بیاورد is the past 'he brought'. چیزی 'something' is the direct object; که introduces a relative clause 'that/which' modifying چیزی. بد خوردنی is a compound in which خوردنی ('edible; fit to be eaten') is qualified by بد ('bad'), so 'bad to eat' or 'not good to eat'. The verb precedes the object here (بیاورد چیزی), a word order acceptable in classical Persian.
segments.s344-g77: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here abbreviated in the senses list as related to speech), and گشتاسپ is the agent; the order 'چنین گفت گشتاسپ' places the adverb and verb before the speaker, a common stylistic inversion. با ساروان 'to/with the caravan leader' marks the addressee using با in an older/epic sense.
segments.s344-g78: که introduces the content clause 'that/which'. این مرد 'this man' is the noun phrase being described. بیدار 'alert/awake' and روشن‌روان 'bright‑souled / clear‑minded' are epithets following the noun (adjectives after the noun, as common in Persian), giving qualities of the man Gushtasp names.
segments.s344-g79: مرا ده is an imperative sequence meaning 'give me' where مرا (me) precedes the imperative ده ('give') — an older/poetic word order (modern Persian more commonly has ده مرا or مرا بده). یکی کاروانی شتر is a noun phrase 'one caravan camel' (یکی = one; کاروانی qualifying as 'of the caravan' or 'for caravan use'; شتر = camel).
segments.s344-g80: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause here 'if/when' (context shows an if: 'if it seems right to you'). رای آیدت literally 'a view/opinion comes to you' = 'if it occurs to you/if you think fit'. مزد ما هم ببر is the main imperative 'take our reward too' (مزد = reward; ما = our/us; هم = also; ببر = take! imperative). The clause structure is conditional + imperative result.
segments.s344-g9: 'به کشتی' uses به as an instrumental 'by' (classical Persian often uses به+noun to indicate means), so 'به کشتی' = 'by ship'. 'برین' is the contracted sequence بر + این (i.e. 'بر این') = 'this/on this'; 'آب' = 'the water'. 'اگر' introduces the conditional ('if'), and 'بگذرم' is 1st person singular present/subjunctive of گذشتن used in the protasis: 'if I cross/pass'. Together: 'If I cross this water by ship.'
segments.s344-g10: The clause is an apodosis/request that follows the condition. 'سپاسی' = 'a (token of) thanks/praise'. 'نهی' here functions as an archaic 2nd-person jussive/imperative meaning 'place/put' (classical Persian ordering often places the object before a short verb). 'جاودان' = 'everlasting/eternal' modifies the praise. 'بر' = 'upon' and 'سرم' = 'my head' (سر + -م). So syntactically it is an imperative/request: 'place eternal praise upon my head.' Note: the supplied senses for نهی and سرم do not match the classical meanings, so those are flagged below.
segments.s344-g11: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus said' (a formulaic remark 'he said thus'). 'شایسته‌ای' is a predicative adjective with the 2nd-person ending -ی (you are worthy / you deserve). 'تاج را' = 'the crown' marked with the object/topic marker را. Word order is typical classical Persian: formulaic verb ('چنین گفت') followed by the predicative phrase.
segments.s344-g12: A coordinated alternative introduced by 'یا' (or). The list 'جوشن و تیغ و تاراج' enumerates possible rewards/items (armor, sword, and plunder), and the final 'را' scopes over the whole list as the definite/object marker. This continues the predicate introduced in the previous segment ('you are worthy of...').
segments.s344-g13: کنون is an adverb meaning 'now' placed at the clause start. راز بگشای is an imperative construction: راز (object) precedes the verb بگشای (archaic/imparative 'reveal, open'); word order Object–Verb is normal in classical Persian. و links the two commands; با من بگوی literally 'speak with me' functions as 'tell me', with با + pronoun (با من) before the verb بگوی (imperative/command form 'say/tell').
segments.s344-g14: ازین سان = از + این + سان ('in/from this manner') = 'in this way'; these three pieces form the adverbial phrase. به دریا گذشتن is the infinitive phrase 'to pass into the sea' (به = into/towards, دریا = sea, گذشتن = to pass/cross). مجوی is a negative jussive/imperative form (do not seek / do not attempt) that governs the infinitive that precedes it; overall: 'do not attempt to cross the sea in this way.'
segments.s344-g15: مرا (here written as م + را) places the beneficiary/affected party ('to me' / 'me') before the predicate. هدیه باید is an impersonal or stative construction: هدیه (a gift/tribute) + باید ('is due / is necessary' / 'must be'), i.e. 'a gift is due to me' or 'I should receive a gift.' اگر گفت راست is a conditional clause 'if (he) speaks/said true' (اگر = if; گفت here refers to speaking/saying — classical usage can be noun or past verb; راست = true), which qualifies the obligation in the main clause.
segments.s344-g16: ترا رای و راه دبیری کجاست is a topicalized interrogative: ترا رای و راه دبیری (your counsel/opinion and the way/method of secretarial office) is fronted, followed by کجاست ('where is it?'). ترا here is the clitic form referring to 'your / to you' attached to the following noun phrase; رای = counsel/opinion/plan, راه دبیری = 'the way/method of secretarial office' (دبیری = chancellery/secretarial practice), and ست is the copula 'is' concluding the question.
segments.s344-g25: 'یکی شارستان بد به روم اندرون' is a classical-Persian clause meaning 'there was a certain town in inner Rome.' یکی is the indefinite 'a certain'; شارستان is the noun 'town/city.' بد here functions as an archaic/poetic past copula (equivalent to 'بود' = 'was') placed after the subject, and به روم اندرون ('in/to inner Rome') is a locative phrase introduced by the preposition به plus اندرون 'inside.'
segments.s344-g26: 'سه فرسنگ پهنای شهرش فزون' gives the measure of the town: literal order is poetic inversion — numeral + unit (سه فرسنگ) precedes the noun phrase پهنای شهرش ('the width/breadth of his/its city'), and the adjective/predicate فزون ('exceeding/abundant') closes the clause, meaning 'the city's breadth exceeded three parasangs.'
segments.s344-g27: 'برآورده سلم جای بزرگ' begins with the past participle/verb برآورده ('brought up' / 'raised') modifying سلم ('ladder / stair'), so the phrase describes that a stair/steps were brought up. جای بزرگ ('a great place/platform') follows as the resulting raised place or dais; the word order places the action (brought up) before the noun it affects for emphasis.
segments.s344-g28: 'نشستنگه قیصران سترگ' names the function of that raised place: نشستنگه is the 'seat' or 'place for sitting,' followed by قیصران ('of the Caesars' — genitive plural), with سترگ functioning as an epithet; together it means 'the seat/place for the great Caesars.' The noun نشستنگه heads the phrase, and the following words specify whose seat it is and its quality.
segments.s344-g29: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گشتاسپ آمد is a simple past clause 'Gushtasp came'. بدان شارستان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/to that city' where ب + دان (classical/poetic form corresponding to به + آن) mark motion toward that place; شارستان = city.
segments.s344-g30: همی is a classical aspectual particle indicating continuous or habitual action (here giving a progressive sense 'was ...ing'). جست is from جستن meaning 'to seek/search' (not 'to leap' here). جای یکی کارستان: جای = 'place', یکی = 'a certain / one' (indefinite), کارستان = 'feat/exploit' — together 'he sought a place for a certain feat' (i.e. looked for a spot/occasion to perform a feat).
segments.s344-g31: همی is an archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual action in past narrative (here 'was ...ing' / 'kept on ...ing') and appears before the verb. گشت is the past 3rd‑person singular of گشتن here meaning 'went/roamed'; the subject is omitted (third person). یک هفته is a duration phrase ('for a week') following the verb, which is common in Persian. بر گرد روم is a prepositional phrase meaning 'around Rome' — گرد 'around/about' combines with the preposition بر and the place name روم (Byzantium) as its object.
segments.s344-g32: همی again marks continuous past action. کار is the direct object 'work' placed before the verb (Persian often puts object(s) before the verb). جست is the past form of جستن here meaning 'sought' or 'looked for'. اندر is the preposition 'in/within' and آباد بوم is an adjectival phrase (آباد 'prosperous/inhabited' modifying بوم 'land, homeland') meaning 'the prosperous land' or 'flourishing realm'. Together the clause means 'he was seeking work in the prosperous land.'
segments.s344-g57: نگه کرد چوپان is a verb‑first, poetic order where نگه (a noun/nominal meaning 'gaze') is combined with the past tense کرد to mean 'kept a look' or simply 'looked'; چوپان follows as the logical subject ('the shepherd'). بنواختش is a past tense verb (بنواخت) with the object enclitic -ش 'it' attached; here it means 'struck/played it' (often used for striking an instrument or object). The two clauses are joined by و 'and'.
segments.s344-g58: به نزدیکی خویش بنشاختش: به is the preposition 'to/at/near'; نزدیکی is the noun 'nearness/close by'; خوی + ش here combine to form خویش 'his/one's own' (the noun plus the enclitic -ش). بنشاختش is a past tense verb with the object enclitic -ش and means 'placed/set (it) down' or 'set it by' — the verb closes the clause and attaches the object pronoun.
segments.s344-g59: چه مردی here functions as an indefinite/introducing phrase ('a certain man' or 'what man'), with چه preceding مردی for a poetic flavor. بدو is the prepositional/enclitic phrase 'to him/unto him'. گفت is the past tense 'said'. با من بگوی is an older/literary request: با من 'with me' + بگوی, a subjunctive/imperative form (classical usage) meaning 'tell/speak to me' — the form -ی- in بگوی is the older subjunctive/hortative shape used to request second‑person action.
segments.s344-g60: که introduces reported speech or a content clause ('that' / 'that you are ...'). هم … و هم is the correlative 'both ... and ...'. شاه functions as the nominal base 'king/royal', modified by شاخی (derived adjective from شاخ 'branch') to mean 'of a branch/line' — idiomatically 'of kingly lineage' here. نامجوی is a compound meaning 'name‑seeking' or 'seeker of renown' (used adjectivally/nominatively).
segments.s344-g61: چنین داد پاسخ is a literary verb phrase: چنین 'thus' + داد (past 'gave') + پاسخ (object 'answer') = 'thus gave answer / answered thus'. که‌ای here is a contracted, classical way of introducing direct address or the reported clause: که + ای (vocative particle) producing 'that, O ...' — immediately followed by the vocative نامدار 'renowned one'.
segments.s344-g62: یکی begins the clause as an indefinite/predicative 'one' or 'a certain (one)'. کره تازم is a compact phrase: کره 'young (here: foal/young horse)' + تازم, where تازم is a present/past‑stative verbal form with first‑person suffix -م meaning 'I gallop/ride swiftly' (i.e. 'I am a swift rider on a young steed'). دلیر و سوار are coordinate predicates/adjectives 'brave and mounted/rider' describing the speaker. Word order is compressed and characteristic of poetry, with predicates stacked after the initial one.
segments.s344-g63: مرا‌گر is a fused conditional phrase equivalent to مرا + گر (where گر = 'if'), meaning 'if (you) toward me' or 'if you favor me'; نوازی here is a second‑person present/subjunctive (or verbal noun sense) 'you favor/you play/you treat kindly' (narrowly 'play/caress' in literal lexical senses but in context 'show kindness'). به کار آیمت: به کار = 'to the work/affair' i.e. 'useful to my affairs'; آیمت is an old/classical verbal form with second‑person ending meaning 'you will come / you will be' or 'you will serve/be of use' — an optative/future sense in context. The whole clause therefore gives a conditional promise.
segments.s344-g64: به رنج و به بد نیز یار آیمت lists the conditions and the promised relationship: به رنج 'in/at suffering', و 'and', به بد 'in ill‑fortune' — نیز 'also' — یار 'companion/ally' — آیمت again the same verbal form 'you will be' or 'you will come' (i.e. 'you will be my companion'). The repeated به + noun is a classical locative/advantage construction ('in hardship, in misfortune'), and یار is predicate after the locatives.
segments.s344-g33: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). در this clause the phrase is structured as a relative 'چیزی که...' = 'that which/whatever there was'; the verb cluster بخورد و بداد is a past narrative: بخورد = 'he ate' (classical past), و = 'and', بداد here is intended as 'he gave', an archaic past form. Word order places the verb phrase at the end of the clause, typical of Persian.
segments.s344-g34: همی is the classical continuous/imperfective particle (here marking the past action 'he went' in narrative style). رفت is the past of 'to go'; ناشاد is an adjective 'unhappy/mournful'; دل پر ز باد is a prepositional phrase 'heart full of (lit.) wind' with پر 'full (of)' followed by ز (poetic 'from/of') and باد 'wind' used metaphorically.
segments.s344-g35: چو introduces another temporal clause ('when/after'); در شهر آباد is a locative phrase 'in the prosperous city'; چندی is an adverbial 'for a while'; the verb بگشت (here classical past of گشتن/برگشتن) comes at the end, 'he wandered/roamed/returned for a while'.
segments.s344-g36: ز ایوان 'from the iwan' (ز = from, poetic form); به دیوان قیصر 'into the court of the Caesar' uses به + place for direction; گذشت is the past 'he passed' and completes the clause, with subject omitted (it is understood as the same 'he').
segments.s344-g37: به اسقف چنین گفت = 'he said thus to the bishop': به اسقف is the indirect object 'to the bishop', چنین گفت 'said thus'. کای دستگیر is a vocative address: کای = 'O' (classical vocative) + دستگیر 'helper/one who succours' as the addressee's epithet.
segments.s344-g38: ز ایران = 'from Iran'; یکی = 'one / a certain' (introducing an indefinite person); نامجویم دبیر here reads as a noun phrase 'a name‑seeking (i.e. fame‑seeking) secretary' — in context the speaker requests a secretary of someone who seeks renown (classical word order places modifiers before the head noun). The verb 'I seek' is implied by the speech context and not explicitly inflected here.
segments.s344-g39: بدین کار = 'in/for this business' (بدین = به + این); باشم is the first-person present/subjunctive form 'I will be / let me be' used here as a declarative 'I will be'; ترا is the clitic form 'to you' (ت = 2nd-person element, را here part of the clitic spelling), and یارمند = 'ally/helper'. The whole phrase means 'I will be your helper in this matter.'
segments.s344-g40: ز دیوان کنم = 'from the divan/court I will make/do' where کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of 'to do/make' used here for a future‑oriented promise; هرچ آید پسند is a relative clause 'whatever comes (آید) [that is] pleasing/acceptable (پسند)', meaning 'I will choose from the court whatever you find acceptable.' Word order places the relative clause after the quantifier هرچ.
segments.s344-g89: برو here is an imperative/lexical verb meaning “go” (or a short reported command) that opens the verbal sequence; آفرین کرد is a light‑verb construction where آفرین (an exclamation/praise) takes کرد as the past “did/made” to mean “he praised/applauded.” The conjunction و links that clause to برگشت “he returned,” and زوی (a contracted poetic form of از وی) attaches to برگشت to mark source/place (“from him/from there”).
segments.s344-g90: پر از غم is a prepositional phrase meaning “full of sorrow” with پر (‘full’) followed by از + noun. سوی شهر is the directional phrase “toward the city.” بنهاد is the past form of نهادن (“to place/set”) used here in a poetic sense with روی (“face”) as its object: بنهاد روی = “he set/turned his face.” Word order places the direction (سوی شهر) before the verb بنهاد in this poetic line.
segments.s344-g91: شد is the copular/auxiliary meaning “became” here; آن دردها is the subject (“those pains”). The prepositional phrase بر دلش (بر + دل + enclitic ش = “upon his heart”) shows the location/target of the suffering, and گران (predicate adjective “heavy”) completes the predication (literally: “those pains became upon his heart heavy”).
segments.s344-g92: بیامد is a simple past verb “came.” The locative phrase به بازار آهنگران (به + place + possessive noun) means “to the market of the smiths”; Persian places such place/complement phrases after the verb in normal word order.
segments.s344-g93: یکی here means “a certain” (introducing someone). نامور is a predicate adjective “renowned”; بود is the past copula “was.” The name follows in apposition (بوراب نام = “named Borāb”), a common classical order in which the label/name comes after the descriptive clause.
segments.s344-g94: پسندیده is an evaluative adjective “esteemed/approved” modifying the following noun آهنگری (“blacksmithing/blacksmith’s craft” or by extension ‘the smith’). شادکام is a following epithet meaning “fortunate” or “blessed with good fortune”; classical Persian often stacks adjectives and epithets after the noun they qualify.
segments.s344-g95: همی is the habitual/continuous marker in classical Persian (meaning “habitually” or “would”), which frames ساختی as a repeated past action (an older/poetic 3rd‑person past). نعل اسپان شاه is the object phrase ‘horseshoes of the king’s steeds’ with the possessed noun (اسپان) followed by the possessor (شاه) — a typical classical genitive order.
segments.s344-g96: بر قیصر is a prepositional phrase ‘upon/against/with respect to the Caesar’; او را is the 3rd‑person pronoun plus the object marker را (topicalized/accusative). پایگاه is the noun “base/place/standing.” The middle word بدی here is archaic/poetic and does not cleanly match the modern glosses given; in context it functions as part of a verbal idea meaning something like ‘gave/caused/placed’ (classical usage), forming “he gave him a standing (before the Caesar).”

sentences.s344-s61: No day of hardship or suffering remained for anyone; there was neither ease and rejoicing, nor treasure.
sentences.s344-s62: Bad and good alike pass over us; whoever has wisdom should not be sullen.
sentences.s344-s33: He said to him, “Do not put this saddle on him; turn him inward — here you are a stranger and a helpless man.”
sentences.s344-s34: The wilderness and the sea and the unbridled horses — how shall I entrust the flock to a stranger?
sentences.s344-s35: When Gushtasp heard (this), he went away sorrowful; he took the route of the Caesar's caravan‑leaders.
sentences.s344-s36: Someone cried 'Bravo!' to the caravan‑leader: 'May you be victorious and of bright spirit.'
sentences.s344-s53: If you accept me, I will help you — I will take charge of that hammer and anvil.
sentences.s344-s54: When Būrāb heard the tale from him, he became his ally.
sentences.s344-s55: The noble utterance leapt into the fire; when it burst into flame, it rushed toward the anvil.
sentences.s344-s56: They gave Gushtāsp a heavy hammer, the smiths having gone to the assembly.
sentences.s344-s49: He had thirty-five bad companions and apprentices who had been brought to hardship by the hammer and the iron.
sentences.s344-s50: Gushtasp sat down in his shop for a long while; that tradesman became weary of sitting.
sentences.s344-s51: He said to him, 'O good‑natured blacksmith, what do you want at our shop? '
sentences.s344-s52: Thus he answered: 'O fortunate one, I will not turn my head away from the hammer nor from the hard work.'
sentences.s344-s9: When Hishoy heard it, Gushtasp said that you had hidden nothing from me.
sentences.s344-s10: As for me, I will not withhold whatever you desire — this helmet, seal, dinars and sword.
sentences.s344-s11: He gave Hishoy a small dinar; from that gift the man who received it became joyful.
sentences.s344-s12: They hoisted the light sail on the ship and drew the world-seeker toward Caesar.
sentences.s344-s41: The caravan-leader said to him, "O brave man, this action never befits you."
sentences.s344-s42: If by anything you truly make yourself our head, and if you set your intention toward the Caesar, ...
sentences.s344-s43: These words will make you self‑sufficient; do nothing except the intention of going to the Caesar's court.
sentences.s344-s44: And if you should lose the road, I have a beast fit (for the journey) and people to guide you.
sentences.s344-s25: When Gushtasp heard, his heart became full of pain; from the court came two pale faces.
sentences.s344-s26: A cold wind rose from his heart; the Caesar came close to the shepherd.
sentences.s344-s27: The young hero's name was Nastāv; he was brave, alert, and radiant.
sentences.s344-s28: When Nastāv drew near, he praised him and led him to prayer.
sentences.s344-s57: He struck the sledge and shattered the anvil, and word of it spread— the bazaar became full of talk.
sentences.s344-s58: Būrāb was frightened and said, 'O young man — iron has no power against your wound.'
sentences.s344-s59: Neither hammer nor fire, neither anvil nor bellows remained; when Gushtasp heard that, he became sullen.
sentences.s344-s60: He threw down the hammer and grew hungry; he neither grimaced at his stew nor set it aside.
sentences.s344-s21: The secretaries who were in the court kept glancing at one another.
sentences.s344-s22: From this steel pen the paper will be made to weep; that very face of the paper will be scorched.
sentences.s344-s23: In a single instant he must be lifted from beneath — by the arm, by the bow, and by the saddle onto the noose.
sentences.s344-s24: They said aloud to us, 'Scribe, it is harmful; coming is inevitable.'
sentences.s344-s1: When Gushtasp came near the sea, he dismounted and saw a cry of entreaty.
sentences.s344-s2: There was an old man named Hishuy — a chivalrous, alert man, full of counsel and desire.
sentences.s344-s3: Gushtasp praised him and said, 'May wisdom be the mate of your pure soul.'
sentences.s344-s4: From Iran I have one renowned scribe, wise, clear‑hearted, and quick to learn.
sentences.s344-s37: When the wise man saw Gushtasp's face, he was welcomed and chose his place.
sentences.s344-s38: He spread a light, open cover and brought something that was bad to eat.
sentences.s344-s39: Thus Gushtasp said to the caravan leader that this man is alert and clear‑minded.
sentences.s344-s40: Give me one caravan camel; if it pleases you, take our reward as well.
sentences.s344-s5: If I cross this water by ship, place an everlasting praise upon my head.
sentences.s344-s6: He said thus: 'You are worthy of the crown — or of armor and sword and spoil.'
sentences.s344-s7: Now unveil the secret and tell me; do not try to cross the sea in this manner.
sentences.s344-s8: If he speaks truly, a gift is due to me; where, then, are your counsel and the way of office/secretarial practice?
sentences.s344-s13: There was a certain town inside (inner) Rome; the breadth of its city was more than three parasangs.
sentences.s344-s14: A stair was brought up — a great platform, the seat of the mighty Caesars.
sentences.s344-s15: When Gushtasp came to that city, he searched for a place for a feat.
sentences.s344-s16: He wandered around Rome for a week, seeking work in the prosperous land.
sentences.s344-s29: The shepherd looked and struck/played it, and placed it down close beside himself.
sentences.s344-s30: A certain man said to him, 'Tell me (tell me this): you are both of kingly stock and a seeker of renown.'
sentences.s344-s31: He answered thus: 'O renowned one, I am one who rides a young steed — brave and a horseman.'
sentences.s344-s32: If you show me favor, you will be useful to my affairs; in hardship and in misfortune you will also be my companion.
sentences.s344-s17: When he had eaten and given away whatever there was, he went off unhappy, his heart full of wind.
sentences.s344-s18: After wandering for a while in the prosperous city, he passed from the iwan into Caesar's court.
sentences.s344-s19: He said to the bishop, 'O helper — fetch me from Iran a certain secretary who seeks renown.'
sentences.s344-s20: For this task I will be your helper; from the court I will (choose) whatever comes pleasing (to you).
sentences.s344-s45: He voiced praise and turned back; from there, full of sorrow, he set his face toward the city.
sentences.s344-s46: Those pains became heavy upon his heart; he came to the smiths’ market.
sentences.s344-s47: There was a certain famed man named Borāb; an approved blacksmith, fortunate.
sentences.s344-s48: He used to make horseshoes for the king’s steeds; for the Caesar he gave him a standing/place.
